body:
It's a bit more complicated.

We don't promise that frpc can always send msg.CloseProxy to frps before exiting. Instead, you should inject logic here https://github.com/fatedier/frp/blob/dev/server/control.go#L375 when we really close and release proxy.

And we should not block on server plugin calls for `CloseProxy` message.  It's better to send notify requests asynchronously. We don't need to wait for plugin's response. It's just a notify message.

For some special cases, such as network error between frpc and frps, it has risk to send all `CloseProxy` messages to plugins. It will create too many connections if there are lots of frpcs and proxies.
author: fatedier

body:
This now works based on my local tests and e2e tests.

> For some special cases, such as network error between frpc and frps, it has risk to send all CloseProxy messages to plugins. It will create too many connections if there are lots of frpcs and proxies.

I think that it's fine, and the plugin itself should handle this situation and aggregate (or delay) the notifications so that it does not trigger rate limits or annoy the user, we shouldn't handle this on our own.
author: kmod-midori

body:
> I think that it's fine, and the plugin itself should handle this situation and aggregate (or delay) the notifications so that it does not trigger rate limits or annoy the user, we shouldn't handle this on our own.

Since we use HTTP/1.1，frps will create new connections for each new concurrent request. It will consume many fds and there are also local ports limit (maybe 30000 - 60000) to the same plugin port.

It's nothing about plugin. frps will get network error in that scenario.
author: fatedier

body:
What about one `CloseProxy` event per `frpc` in `stoper`? We can have a `ProxyNames []string` field for this. Aggregating the shutdown of multiple `frpc`s seems difficult.

Since it is still possible for a client to close a single proxy by sending `CloseProxy` messages, we need to handle this case as well, put a single name in that array.

body:
需求和理由
1、暴露服务到公网的需求：教会所有rdp/ssh等用户去使用stcp，并不现实。比如你的老板，导师，以及很少使用这些功能的朋友，同事。因此总会暴露部分服务到公网端口上，并承受相关风险。
2、服务端防护？Windows端的暴力破解防护，不知道在哪，日志发现，每隔2-3分钟就会出现一次rdp登录错误，持续几个小时，window防火墙没有丝毫反应。sshd端防护的话，需要proxy_protocol外加 mmproxy，配置繁杂。
3、frps服务器端，利用fail2ban 检测那些重复性的“持续时间很短”的frp连接，屏蔽容易很多，而且屏蔽掉那些暴力破解流量后，frps的资源占用也会少很多。

I misunderstood the previous question a little bit. It's more difficult to support on server side.

Your change can't take effect for all proxy types, udp & http? They don't call `HandleUserTCPConnection` function.

And it should not take effect both on client and server side. It's a bit of a waste of resources.

I need to consider more about this feature later.
author: fatedier

body:
> I misunderstood the previous question a little bit. It's more difficult to support on server side.
> 
> Your change can't take effect for all proxy types, udp & http? They don't call `HandleUserTCPConnection` function.
> 
> And it should not take effect both on client and server side. It's a bit of a waste of resources.
> 
> I need to consider more about this feature later.

Thank you for the reply @fatedier, I see what you mean for other proxy types, I can investigate that and update the PR.

As for client and server setting this and wasting resources, is that the same for encryption and compression?  If the client controls the server, then they know whether or not it will be set and can avoid wasting their own resources.  If they do not control the server, then there is no way of knowing for sure.

Thinking about the possibilities if the client _does not_ control the server, for example:
* If the server sets a 10MB limit, but a client sets a 5MB limit, then 5MB will be the limit - client is able to additionally control the lower limit, if necessary
* If the server sets a 10MB limit, but a client sets a 20MB limit, then 10MB will be the limit - server is able to enforce the upper limit

There would be some wasted resources in the above scenarios, but there is also some additional functionality and control that can be achieved by each separate party (client and server).

Let me know if I can help out in any way.

body:
@blizard863 Do you have time to help fix and test this issue? In the `ctl.authSetter.SetPing` in the client, if it fails, it should continue to the next iteration instead of exiting directly. This will cause the entire message processing loop to exit.

In addition, OIDC and token are different here. The time required here is unpredictable, so it is necessary to start a new goroutine to handle it instead of synchronous processing.

author: NanamiNakano

body:
I have some simple ideas about refactoring in configuration management, but they are not perfect. There will likely be significant changes in this area in the future. Without clear specifications, I am currently not inclined to add any extra functionality.

Currently, after you have modified the configuration file, you can use the `frpc reload` command to reload the configuration file, or use the functionality provided on the web page to modify the configuration and reload. Compared to introducing the inotify API, I believe these are already sufficient.

You can also use another tool to watch for file updates and then call the reload interface of frpc. This combination may also be more in line with the philosophy of Unix systems.

body:
Thanks for your contribution.

However, for such functionality, we need to be more cautious in our design and thinking. We usually do not tend to directly submit code, but rather submit proposals and specific implementation paths in the issue, discuss and reach a consensus before attempting to code, in order to avoid wasting valuable time due to inconsistencies with project planning.

There has been no progress on this issue for various reasons. One reason is that I am not clear on how useful this functionality is in the current network environment. This capability may require testing and long-term maintenance in various network environments, which would increase the project's maintenance costs. We should only consider it when the benefits are clearly greater than the added costs. Additionally, during the transition period of major version refactoring, we hope to minimize the complexity brought by new features.
author: fatedier

body:
Thank you very much for your prompt and timely response. I understand and agree with your concerns.

With the development of technologies such as P2P, initiating UPnP requests and asking routers to cooperate is a common technique in scenarios requiring public network mapping. Investigation and testing in this context, as outlined in #1823, demonstrate that even within ISP-level NAT networks, some providers offer support for related technologies.

In IPv6 environments, even though each endpoint can acquire an IPv6 address, most routers currently block all inbound requests from the internet for security reasons. Therefore, it's still necessary to configure the opening of corresponding ports. The use of UPnP in these scenarios retains significant value.

Moreover, some tools also offer similar functionalities, like ZeroTier (https://www.zerotier.com/).

The current implementation is a straightforward extension of the STUN implementation for xTCP.

After acquiring a public IP address, a callback is invoked. If permitted by the configuration, the UPnP feature is used to direct the router to transfer the externally exposed port to an open UDP port, thereby "creating" an EasyNAT in this environment. The subsequent process fully reuses the STUN procedure.

I haven't closely examined the current implementation, but my assumption is that either the visitor or the proxy needs to be reachable by the other party for a direct connection to be established without the need for server relay. Therefore, I have implemented related configuration code on both the visitor and the proxy sides.

If there's any misunderstanding on my part, please kindly correct me.

Below is a log output from a proxy with this feature enabled, for your reference.


Just created a test build from `dev` and it looks like the metadatas are not being passed to the server.

Client:

```bash
frp/bin/frpc http -s <server> -d <domain> --user <user> --metadatas token=<token>
2025-01-06 18:30:55.728 [I] [client/service.go:295] try to connect to server...
2025-01-06 18:30:55.811 [E] [client/service.go:280] send Login request to plugin error
2025-01-06 18:30:55.811 [W] [client/service.go:298] connect to server error: send Login request to plugin error
login to the server failed: send Login request to plugin error. With loginFailExit enabled, no additional retries will be attempted
```

Server plugin shows the following payload and therefore is unable to validate the token.

```json
{
  "version": "0.61.1",
  "os": "linux",
  "arch": "amd64",
  "user": "<user>",
  "privilege_key": "3a108ca101d3bea67f6892ec6ee7e82a",
  "timestamp": 1736184655,
  "client_spec": {},
  "pool_count": 1,
  "client_address": "<client-ip:client-port>"
}
````
author: morph027

body:
It's metadata for proxy, not for client.

Command-line arguments are not very convenient for expressing complex hierarchical structures, so supporting client metadata may not be considered.

## Walkthrough

This set of changes spans multiple files and modules, focusing primarily on two areas: (1) standardizing log message capitalization for consistency across the codebase, and (2) significant internal refactoring and enhancement of the virtual network (vnet) and TUN device handling. The vnet changes introduce improved connection and route lifecycle management, batch reading for TUN devices, and dynamic naming for TUN interfaces. Other files are updated to ensure that log messages consistently use lowercase initial letters, without modifying any functional logic or exported interfaces except where new fields or methods are needed for the vnet enhancements.

## Changes

| File(s)                                                                                          | Change Summary                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| client/admin_api.go, client/control.go, client/service.go, server/control.go, server/dashboard_api.go, server/service.go, pkg/util/vhost/http.go, pkg/util/vhost/vhost.go, test/e2e/e2e.go, test/e2e/framework/request.go, test/e2e/legacy/features/real_ip.go, test/e2e/v1/features/real_ip.go | Standardized log messages to use lowercase initial letters (e.g., "http", "pong", "new control", "replaced by client", etc.) for consistency. No changes to logic, error handling, or control flow.                                                                                                                |
| pkg/plugin/client/virtual_net.go                                                                 | Refactored connection management: added mutex and map to track active connections, implemented explicit cleanup and removal of connections, replaced registration/unregistration with lifecycle tracking and safe concurrent handling. Added `RemoveConn` method and updated `Close` method for robust cleanup.                                                                                                               |
| pkg/plugin/visitor/virtual_net.go                                                                | Simplified client route registration: removed error handling and retry logic, eliminated cleanup of pipe pairs on registration failure, and adjusted log message casing. The core connection lifecycle remains, but complexity around registration errors is reduced.                                                                                                                                    |
| pkg/vnet/controller.go                                                                           | Major refactor for connection and route lifecycle: improved cleanup of client/server routes and IP mappings, introduced `StartServerConnReadLoop` with cleanup callback, removed registration methods, optimized locking, and added new methods for cleanup. Updated method signatures and internal structures to support these changes.                                                                |
| pkg/vnet/message.go                                                                              | Changed error formatting in `ReadMessage` and `WriteMessage` to use `%w` for proper error wrapping, enabling Go error unwrapping and inspection. Logic and messages unchanged.                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| pkg/vnet/tun.go                                                                                  | Enhanced TUN device wrapper: added buffer management fields, implemented batch reading with internal buffering, adjusted buffer sizing based on MTU, and updated read/write logic for efficiency. Struct and method implementations updated accordingly.                                                                                                                                            |
| pkg/vnet/tun_linux.go                                                                            | Implemented dynamic TUN device naming: selects next available device name or generates a fallback using a hash of the address. Added helper functions for naming, improved error handling, and updated interface setup to use actual device names. Renamed constant for clarity.                                                                              |

## Sequence Diagram(s)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Plugin as VirtualNetPlugin
    participant Controller
    participant Conn as Connection

    Plugin->>Plugin: Add Conn to conns map (mutex lock)
    Plugin->>Controller: StartServerConnReadLoop(Conn, cleanup callback)
    Controller->>Conn: Read loop starts
    Note right of Controller: On Conn close or error
    Controller->>Plugin: Invoke cleanup callback
    Plugin->>Plugin: Remove Conn from conns map (mutex lock)
    Plugin->>Conn: Close Conn
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Controller
    participant TunDevice
    participant App as Application

    App->>TunDevice: Read(p []byte)
    alt packetBuffers not empty
        TunDevice->>App: Return buffered packet
    else
        TunDevice->>TunDevice: Batch read packets into readBuffers
        TunDevice->>TunDevice: Buffer packets in packetBuffers
        TunDevice->>App: Return first buffered packet
    end

    App->>TunDevice: Write(p []byte)
    TunDevice->>TunDevice: Allocate buffer (offset + bufferSize)
    TunDevice->>TunDevice: Copy p into buffer at offset
    TunDevice->>TunDevice: Write buffer to device
    TunDevice->>App: Return bytes written
```

## Poem

> In fields of code, a rabbit hops,
> Lowercase logs—no sudden stops!
> Connections tracked with gentle care,
> TUNs named fresh from springtime air.
> Batch reads nibble, errors wrap tight,
> The vnet garden grows just right.
> 🐇✨ Hooray for tidy code tonight!

body:
> ### WHY --> [Feature Request](https://github.com/fatedier/frp/issues/5185)
> When `auth.additionalScopes` includes `HeartBeats`, every heartbeat calls `clientcredentials.Config.Token(ctx)` which creates a new `oauth2.ReuseTokenSource` on each invocation — the token is never actually reused between calls. This causes a redundant HTTP request to the OIDC token endpoint on every heartbeat interval.
> 
> ### Changes
> * Use `Config.TokenSource(ctx)` once at init to create a persistent `oauth2.TokenSource`
>   that caches the token and only refreshes when it approaches expiry
> * Wrap with `oauth2.ReuseTokenSourceWithExpiry` for a configurable early-refresh buffer
> * Add `tokenRefreshAdvanceDuration` config option (default: 300s) to control how
>   early before expiry the token is refreshed
> * Add unit test with mock HTTP server verifying token is fetched only once across
>   multiple login and ping calls
> 
> Client Config
> 
> ```
> [auth]
> method = "oidc"
> additionalScopes = ["HeartBeats"]
> 
> [auth.oidc]
> clientID = "my-client"
> clientSecret = "my-secret"
> tokenEndpointURL = "https://idp.example.com/oauth/token"
> 
> [[proxies]]
> name = "my-proxy"
> type = "https"
> ...
> ```
> 
> ## Test plan
> * [x]  Added `TestOidcAuthProviderCachesToken` with mock HTTP server proving only 1 token request is made across login + 5 pings
> * [x]  All existing auth tests pass (`go test ./pkg/auth/...`)
> * [x]  `go vet ./pkg/auth/...` clean
> * [x]  `go build` compiles cleanly
> * [x]  Manual testing with OIDC provider and `auth.additionalScopes = ["HeartBeats"]` — verified token is reused across heartbeats and refreshed only near expiry
author: shani1998

body:
Thank you for this PR. The caching optimization for the OIDC token source is a great improvement. However, I've identified a regression that affects OIDC providers that do not return an expires_in field in their token response.

In the golang.org/x/oauth2 library, when a token's Expiry is the zero value (which happens when expires_in is missing), it is treated as valid forever. This means tokenSource.Token() will continue to return the initial cached token indefinitely and will never attempt to refresh it. This is a critical issue because, even though the expires_in field is missing, the underlying JWT will still have its own exp claim. Once that time passes, the server-side OIDC verification will fail for operations like ping or establishing new work connections, as FRP will still be using the stale token.

The previous implementation, which called tokenGenerator.Token(ctx) on every request, did not have this dependency on expires_in and always fetched a fresh token, avoiding this problem.

To maintain compatibility with all OIDC providers, I suggest we add a fallback mechanism:
1. After creating the cached TokenSource, check if the initial token's Expiry is the zero value.
2. If it is, this indicates the provider did not return expires_in. In this case, we should fall back to the original behavior of not using the cached TokenSource and instead fetch a new token for each request.


This will ensure the optimization works for the majority of cases while preventing a regression for providers that follow the OAuth 2.0 spec by omitting the recommended expires_in field.

body:
Thanks for the contribution! Found a couple of issues:

### 1. `pkg/util/http/http.go` — `ParseBasicAuth` behavioral change

The manual `strings.Cut` simplification drops the `!ok` guard:

```go
// Before (original)
cs := string(c)
s := strings.IndexByte(cs, ':')
if s < 0 {
    return  // returns ("", "", false)
}
return cs[:s], cs[s+1:], true

// This PR
return strings.Cut(string(c), ":")
```

When the decoded string has no `:`, `strings.Cut` returns `(fullString, "", false)` — so `username` is no longer empty. Both callers (`pkg/util/vhost/http.go:275` and `pkg/util/tcpmux/httpconnect.go:65`) ignore the `ok` return value with `_`, which means a malformed `Proxy-Authorization` header could affect route matching.

The `golangci-lint --fix` auto-generated version is actually correct and preserves the guard:

```go
before, after, ok0 := strings.Cut(cs, ":")
if !ok0 {
    return
}
return before, after, true
```

This also matches the stdlib's own `parseBasicAuth` implementation in `net/http/request.go`.

### 2. `Makefile` — `make fix` target

`go fix -omitzero=false` is not a valid flag — `go fix` only accepts `-fix`. This causes `make fix` to always fail. Please don't add the `fix` target to the Makefile for now.

### 3. General request

Please don't mix manual modifications with auto-fix changes in the same commit. The linter auto-fix output should be committed as-is, and any manual optimizations (e.g. `maps.Clone` instead of `maps.Copy`, inlining `SplitSeq`, simplifying `WaitGroup.Go`) should be in a separate commit. This makes it much easier to review and verify correctness.

body:
Hi, NewTokenVerifierFromStatic can call DecodeJWKS with a nil *JSONWebKeySet, which dereferences jwks.Keys and panics, potentially crashing the server at startup when the dynamic provider lookup fails and static config is incomplete.

**Severity:** action required | **Category:** reliability

**How to fix:** Validate IssuerSpec and nil-check

**Agent prompt to fix** - you can give this to your LLM of choice:

> ### Issue description
> `NewTokenVerifierFromStatic` may call `DecodeJWKS(nil)` which panics because `DecodeJWKS` dereferences `jwks.Keys` without a nil guard.
> 
> ### Issue Context
> This is reachable when `oidc.NewProvider` fails and the code falls back to static verification; if the user didn’t provide `issuerSpec.jwksFile`, `issuerSpec.pemFile`, or inline `issuerSpec.jwks`, the server can crash.
> 
> ### Fix Focus Areas
> - pkg/auth/oidc.go[288-311]
> - pkg/auth/utils.go[53-59]
> 
> ### Expected fix
> - Return a clear error if no static key source is configured (or if `IssuerSpec.JWKS == nil`).
> - Optionally make `DecodeJWKS` return an error (or empty slice + error) instead of panicking on nil.
> - Consider rejecting empty decoded key lists early (before creating verifier).

We noticed a couple of other issues in this PR as well - happy to share if helpful.

---

body:
Thanks for the contribution, but I have to decline this PR.

The reported "CRITICAL" vulnerability does not actually exist. Let me explain:

**The threat model in the PR description doesn't hold up:**

1. `AuthOIDCServerConfig.Issuer` is set by the frps administrator in the server config file. It is **not** an attacker-controllable input. If an attacker can modify the frps config, they already have full control of the server — there is no privilege escalation here.

2. `SkipIssuerCheck` defaults to `false`. This is an explicit, documented opt-in for administrators with legitimate use cases (e.g. OIDC providers whose `iss` claim does not match the discovery URL exactly, certain multi-tenant setups). It is not an insecure default.

3. Even when `SkipIssuerCheck=true`, token signatures are still verified against keys fetched from the admin-configured issuer's JWKS endpoint over TLS. The `iss` string comparison being skipped does not bypass signature verification.

4. The "DNS hijacking" scenario is mitigated by standard TLS certificate validation in `createOIDCHTTPClient`, which is unrelated to `SkipIssuerCheck`.

**The PR also introduces a real regression:**

Hardcoding `SkipIssuerCheck: false` while leaving the config field, its documentation (`pkg/config/v1/server.go`), and the example config (`conf/frps_full_example.toml`) untouched means existing users who legitimately rely on this option will silently break with no error and no deprecation path. The kilo-code-bot review correctly flagged this.

**A broader request:**

Please do not open PRs that are generated entirely by automated AI security scanners without first analyzing and understanding the finding yourself. These tools frequently flag configuration knobs as vulnerabilities without considering the threat model, the trust boundary, or the legitimate use cases. They produce confident-sounding reports that look like security work but actually create noise for maintainers and risk landing real regressions (like this one) under a "security fix" label.

If you believe there is a genuine issue with how frp validates OIDC tokens, please open an issue describing the concrete attack scenario — who the attacker is, what they control, and what they gain — and we can discuss it from there.

Closing this PR.
author: fatedier

body:
Hi, `dispatch()` performs blocking stdin/stdout I/O while holding `p.mu` and has no timeouts/cancellation, so a stalled child can hang one request indefinitely and block all subsequent requests; it can also prevent `Close()` from acquiring the mutex to kill the child during shutdown.

**Severity:** action required | **Category:** reliability

**How to fix:** Add timeouts and cancellation

**Agent prompt to fix** - you can give this to your LLM of choice:

> ### Issue description
> `dispatch()` holds a global mutex while doing unbounded blocking reads from the child’s stdout. If the child never produces a newline, requests hang indefinitely and all subsequent requests to the plugin block behind the mutex; `Close()` can also block waiting for the same mutex.
> 
> ### Issue Context
> The HTTP handler currently does not use `r.Context()`, and the child process is started without a context/deadline. `http.Server` only sets `ReadHeaderTimeout`, so body reads and backend stdio waits can last forever.
> 
> ### Fix Focus Areas
> - pkg/plugin/client/mcp_stdio.go[111-181]
> - pkg/plugin/client/mcp_stdio.go[203-255]
> - pkg/plugin/client/mcp_stdio.go[313-321]
> 
> ### What to change
> - Stop holding `p.mu` across blocking I/O (e.g., implement a single worker goroutine that owns the child pipes and processes requests via a channel, or lock only to swap/check child state and do I/O outside the lock).
> - Plumb request cancellation into dispatch (e.g., accept a `context.Context` from `handle()` and use it to bound waiting for stdout).
> - Add read/write deadlines for the child I/O and/or a per-request timeout to fail fast and respawn/kill the child on timeout.
> - Ensure `Close()` can always terminate quickly (e.g., kill the child before waiting on anything that could require acquiring `p.mu`, or make the worker goroutine observe a close signal).

We noticed a couple of other issues in this PR as well - happy to share if helpful.

---
*Found by [Qodo](https://github.com/marketplace/qodo-merge-pro-for-open-source) code review. FYI, Qodo is free for open-source.*
author: Qodo-Free-For-OSS

body:
Thanks for the review. The concerns are valid — the original implementation held the global mutex across blocking I/O, which could stall all requests if the child process hung, and prevented `Close()` from acquiring the lock during shutdown.

Addressed in the latest commit by refactoring to a worker goroutine pattern:
- A single `worker()` goroutine exclusively owns the child process and all its I/O — no mutexes needed
- `dispatch()` communicates with the worker via channels and respects `r.Context()` for cancellation throughout
- A 30-second per-request read timeout kills and respawns the child on a stalled response (the goroutine spawned for the read never leaks since the buffered channel absorbs its result after the pipe closes)
- `Close()` simply signals `closeCh` and waits on `workerDone` — no lock contention possible
- Idle reaping is merged into the worker's `select` loop, removing the separate `reapLoop` goroutine

body:
Thanks for the detailed review. All four warnings addressed in the latest commit:

**1. Body size limit** — wrapped `r.Body` with `http.MaxBytesReader(1 MiB)` before `io.ReadAll`.

**2. Replay handshake blocking** — extracted a `readLine` helper closure (goroutine + buffered channel + `time.After` select) and replaced the bare `childOut.ReadBytes` in the `ensureChild` replay path.

**3. Pretty-printed JSON splitting frames** — added `json.Compact` in `handle()` after reading the body; multi-line input is collapsed to a single line before being forwarded to the child's stdin.

**4. Response not matched by id** — added a `readResponse` helper that loops, discarding lines whose JSON-RPC `id` does not match the request's `id`, until the correct response arrives. The MCP spec ([transports](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic/transports)) explicitly permits servers to send notifications (e.g. `notifications/progress`) before the response to a request, so reading only the next line was a protocol-level bug. A single `time.After` deadline is shared across iterations so chatty servers cannot extend the timeout.

body:
I don't think we should further extend the current `http_proxy` environment-variable behavior.

In frpc, this proxy setting only applies to the frpc-to-frps connection, while frpc may also connect to local services or other endpoints. Using a process-wide environment variable for this makes the scope ambiguous.

I prefer making this explicit via `transport.proxyURL`. We may also consider weakening or deprecating the automatic fallback from `http_proxy` in the future.
author: fatedier

body: That's a fair point — a process-wide environment variable is an ambiguous scope for what is really just the frpc-to-frps connection, and steering users toward an explicit `transport.proxyURL` (and eventually phasing out the `http_proxy` fallback) is a cleaner direction than extending the env-var behavior. Closing this out. Thanks for the thoughtful review!
author: Rilomilo
